title: Rush plait table runner
subtitle: A plaited bulrush runner for a dining or kitchen table
excerpt: A table runner plaited from dried bulrush stems: 120 cm long and 30 cm wide, with a finished border. Bulrush plait is the same technique used in rush matting and seating; this runner is a shorter introduction to the pattern. The plait uses groups of three stems, worked in a flat braid.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for rush matting and plaiting; Victorian domestic craft manuals for rush plait forms.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried stems of bulrush (Typha latifolia or Schoenoplectus lacustris) used for plaiting and weaving. Rush must be soaked before use to restore flexibility.
A basketry and mat-making technique in which stems are interlaced in a repeating diagonal pattern. In rush work, plaiting uses groups of two or three stems as a single unit.

content.text: Rush
plaiting

is the simplest of the rush crafts: stems grouped and interlaced in a flat braid, then the braid coiled or stitched to form a mat or runner. The technique needs no frame, no bodkin, and no soaking trough; a bowl of water and a pair of secateurs is the complete toolkit. A
rush
table runner is a natural starting point before moving on to round mats or rush seating.
The rush

Cut bulrush (Typha latifolia, the common bulrush with the brown seed head) in late summer when the stems are full and the seed heads are firm. Cut at water level; the full stem length is needed. Dry the stems in bundles laid flat in a dry shaded place for 3 to 4 weeks before use. Before plaiting, soak the dried stems in cold water for 20 minutes; they will become flexible without becoming waterlogged.
Method
Group the soaked stems into bunches of three; each bunch is one plait unit. Sort by thickness; thicker stems for the outer rows, thinner stems for the middle.
Lay 8 groups side by side (24 stems); this forms the starting width of the runner at 30 cm. Tie the top ends together temporarily with a spare stem.

Plait across the width: take a group from the right, pass it over the next group to the left, under the next, over the next. Work left to right and right to left alternately. Press each row down firmly before starting the next; loose plaiting will loosen further when dry.
Join new stem groups when the working ends become too short to continue: taper the old ends and overlap the new stems by 6 to 8 cm before starting the next plait row.
Continue to 120 cm. Finish by tucking the last row's ends back under the preceding two rows; no knots required. Trim any protruding ends with secateurs after the runner has dried.
Finishing
Lay the finished runner flat on a clean surface to dry. Rush shrinks slightly as it dries and the plait will tighten; do not try to correct the width while wet. Once dry, the runner is ready to use.
Variations
Round mat: the same plait stitched into a coil with a linen thread.
Wider runner: add 4 more groups (12 stems) for a 45 cm width.
Care

Wipe with a damp cloth to clean. Avoid prolonged wetting; standing water will soften the rush and loosen the plait. When the runner becomes dry and brittle after several years, mist it lightly with water and allow to dry flat.

items.symptom: Plait is uneven width; narrower at one end than the other
items.cause: The stem groups are being pulled to one side during plaiting, or groups were dropped without replacement
items.fix: Count the groups at each row end; the count must remain constant. Keep the working surface flat and weighted; a board on top of the completed rows keeps them from shifting.

items.symptom: Stems split and crack during plaiting
items.cause: Insufficient soaking; or stems soaked but then allowed to dry before use
items.fix: Re-soak the stems if they have dried out during a long session. Work in short batches and re-wet stems every 30 to 40 minutes in a hot room.
